narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and a respectable LSAT score of 35, you brought some strong academic credentials to your law school application, along with your plan to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 40.4%, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize candidates with lower predicted risks. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the lower tier compared to peers who typically succeed on the bar. Nearly balancing this out was your undergraduate GPA, which had an equally strong but opposite pull toward predicting success, as it exceeded the norm and suggested solid overall preparation. Your LSAT score also nudged things positively, though with somewhat less force, since 35 is close to but slightly under the average of 36.7 seen in bar passers. Your middle family income bracket played a neutral role here, without swaying the outcome much either way. In essence, while your GPA and LSAT showed promise akin to successful students, the sharp drop-off in your Year 3 decile stood out as the top concern, making the model view your bar passage odds as riskier than average. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many from similar backgrounds thrive—but a data-driven flag on that specific performance dip. To move forward, consider retaking courses or building stronger late-stage grades to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through a post-bac program or targeted test prep that aligns with bar demands. You could also seek letters from professors highlighting your resilience and growth trajectory. Reapplying after addressing that Year 3 weakness could flip the model's view, opening doors at schools that value your unique story. Keep pushing; your above-average GPA proves you've got the foundation to turn this around.
